U.S. Economic Bubble Watch:

May 22 - Wall Street Journal (Nick Timiraos): "An economy facing possible indigestion from big increases in tariffs now is contending with a second headwind: potential fallout from a rise in government borrowing costs. Congressional Republicans and the White House have plowed ahead with a package of tax cuts designed to shore up the economy and soothe jitters about the hit from tariffs. But the cost of the tax bill could be adding to investor concerns over perpetually high budget deficits. Longer-dated government bonds continued their selloff on Thursday morning after Republicans successfully pushed President Trump's tax bill through the House of Representatives. Yields rise as bond prices fall."

May 22 - Reuters (Lucia Mutikani): "U.S. business activity picked up in May amid a truce in the trade war between Washington and China, but President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs on imported goods raised prices for companies and consumers. The survey from S&P Global… hinted at an acceleration in inflation in the coming months and a labor market slowdown… Manufacturing delivery delays were the longest in 31 months while exports of services, including spending by foreign visitors in the U.S., dropped at the sharpest pace since the COVID-19 pandemic… The survey's flash manufacturing PMI increased to 52.3 from 50.2 in the prior month… Its flash services PMI rose to 52.3 from 50.8 in April… A measure of prices paid by businesses for inputs vaulted to 63.4, the highest level since November 2022, from 58.5 in April… The survey's gauge of prices charged by businesses for goods and services jumped to 59.3, the highest since August 2022, from 54.0 in April."

May 22 - Associated Press (Matt Ott): "The number of Americans filing unemployment claims last week fell slightly as businesses continue to retain employees despite growing economic uncertainty over U.S. trade policy. Applications for jobless benefits fell by 2,000 to 227,000 for the week ending May 17… The total number of Americans receiving unemployment benefits for the week of May 10 climbed by 36,000 to 1.9 million."

May 22 - Reuters (Lucia Mutikani): "U.S. existing home sales unexpectedly fell in April… Home sales slipped 0.5% last month to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.00 million units… Sales last month were the slowest for April since 2009… They declined 2.0% on a year-over-year basis in April. 'Home sales have been at 75% of normal or pre-pandemic activity for the past three years,' said Lawrence Yun, the NAR's chief economist… With sales being weak, the inventory of existing homes increased 9.0% to 1.45 million units in April. Supply soared 20.8% from a year ago. An oversupply of new homes on the market could pull buyers away from previously owned ones. Builders are cutting prices and offering incentives to attract customers."

May 21 - CNBC (Diana Olick): "After shifting in a narrow range for several weeks, mortgage rates moved decidedly higher last week… 'Mortgage rates jumped to their highest level since February last week, with investors concerned about rising inflation and the impact of increasing deficits and debt,' said Mike Fratantoni, senior vice president and chief economist at the MBA. Applications for a mortgage to buy a home, which had been rising for a few weeks, dropped 5% for the week and were 13% higher than the same week one year ago."

China Watch:

May 20 - Bloomberg: "China's fiscal stimulus pushed its four-month budget deficit to a record high, as the government ramped up support for the economy during an escalation in its trade conflict with the US. The broad deficit reached 2.65 trillion yuan ($367bn) in January-April, the most ever for the period… The shortfall swelled by more than 50% compared with a year earlier. It's the clearest evidence yet that Beijing shifted into a higher gear in deploying this year's planned fiscal stimulus…"

May 19 - Bloomberg: "Major Chinese banks cut deposit rates again… Lenders including Industrial & Commercial Bank of China Ltd., China Construction Bank Corp., Agricultural Bank of China, Bank of China Ltd. and China Merchants Bank Co. trimmed rates across maturities on Tuesday. One- and two-year fixed deposit rates fell by 15 bps… Rates on three- and five-year deposits were slashed by 25 bps. The cuts on the country's more 300 trillion yuan ($41.6 trillion) of deposits came as the recent package of government measures to stimulate the world's second largest economy has further squeezed bank profits."

May 18 - Reuters (Liangping Gao, Xiuhao Chen and Liz Lee): "China's new home prices were unchanged in April from a month earlier…, extending the no-growth trend to nearly two years despite policymakers' efforts to stabilise the sector. New home prices stayed the same for a second straight month and have shown no growth since May 2023 as China attempts to lift the real estate sector, once a key driver of the economy, from a prolonged slump. 'The property sector remains on a downward path. While many believe we're approaching the bottom, the exact distance remains uncertain,' said Zhaopeng Xing, senior China strategist at ANZ. From a year earlier, prices in April were down 4.0%..."

May 18 - CNBC (Anniek Bao and Evelyn Cheng): "China's retail sales growth slowed in April…, signaling that consumption remains a worry for the world's second-largest economy. Retail sales rose 5.1% from a year earlier in April, missing analysts' estimates of 5.5% growth… Sales had grown by 5.9% in the previous month. Industrial output grew 6.1% year-on-year in April…"

May 20 - Bloomberg (David Fickling): "Casting your mind back to the China of 2003 almost feels like an exercise in historical fiction. With an economy barely larger than Italy's, it was still underdeveloped and isolated… And yet the China of 2025 is oddly reminiscent of a generation ago. With a four-year real estate crash showing few signs of abating, construction of new housing is now back to the levels of the early 2000s. Just 132 million square meters of homes have been started in the four months through April… That's less than a third of the level before the crash began in 2021, and the lowest total since 2003."

Central Bank Watch:

May 20 - Bloomberg (Jesús Aguado): "Geopolitical and trade tensions stemming from U.S. tariffs pose a risk to the stability of the global financial system and to global economic growth, European Central Bank policymaker Jose Luis Escriva warned… Escriva saw the unpredictability of the Trump administration's policies, from trade to wider economy, as one of the risks defining the global environment, with 'possible deterioration of international investors' confidence in the U.S. economy' also generating uncertainty. 'All these factors have a decisive impact on the global growth outlook and pose risks to the stability of the international financial system,' Escriva, who also chairs the Bank of Spain, wrote in its annual report."

May 20 - Bloomberg (Mark Schroers): "The European Central Bank warned that escalating global tariff tensions have emerged as a 'significant concern' for financial stability and the global economy. 'Policy authorities need to identify the risks stemming from trade tensions, monitor them and evaluate their potential impact on financial stability,' six staff economists wrote… The ECB recommended that 'financial institutions should also take a number of proactive steps to cope with risks stemming from trade tensions.'"

Europe Watch:

May 19 - Associated Press (Sylvia Hui): "Britain and the European Union hailed a new chapter in their relationship… after sealing fresh agreements on defense cooperation and easing trade flows at their first formal summit since Brexit. Five years after the U.K. left the EU, ties were growing closer again as Prime Minister Keir Starmer met European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and other senior EU officials… The deals will slash red tape, grow the British economy and reset relations with the 27-nation trade bloc, Starmer said, while von der Leyen called the talks a 'historic moment' that benefits both sides. 'Britain is back on the world stage,' Starmer told reporters. 'This deal is a win-win.'"

May 21 - Associated Press (Pan Pylas): "Inflation in the U.K. spiked to its highest level for more than a year in April amid a raft of higher domestic bills, such as energy and water… The Office for National Statistics said that its key measure of inflation… rose by 3.5% in the year to April, up from 2.6% in March. April's rate was the highest since January 2024 and above expectations for a more modest increase to 3.3%. The scale of the rise was also the largest since October 2022, at the height of the energy crisis in the wake of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine."

May 19 - Financial Times (Marton Dunai): "Pro-EU centrist Nicuşor Dan won Romania's presidential run-off…, defeating an ultranationalist Eurosceptic who had topped the first round and raised fears about the country's pro-western orientation… The mayor's victory caps months of political and economic turmoil triggered by the cancellation of a previous vote owing to alleged Russian interference, which put Bucharest in Moscow's and Washington's crosshairs and split the nation over its way forward."

Japan Watch:

May 22 - CNBC (Anniek Bao): "Japan's core inflation accelerated to 3.5% in April… Headline inflation climbed 3.6% from a year ago, steady from the prior month and staying above the Bank of Japan's 2% target for more than three years."

May 22 - Associated Press (Mari Yamaguchi): "Rice is essential to Japanese culture, tradition and politics. People take pride in the oval-shaped sticky Japonica grain, which is still a staple even though total consumption has fallen over the decades. But since last summer, prices have soared as supplies have fallen short of demand… To cope with shortfalls this year, the government has released rice reserves. But the grain has been slow to reach supermarket shelves. Anger over that was part of the reason the agriculture minister quit this week."

May 20 - Financial Times (Harry Dempsey, David Keohane and Leo Lewis): "Japan's largest companies have said tariffs planned by the Trump administration could erode annual profits by tens of billions of dollars, with the probability of a bigger impact in the event of a US recession. Groups including Toyota, Sony and Mizuho could suffer a total hit as high as ¥4tn ($27.6bn), according to Financial Times calculations based on company guidance…"

Emerging Market Watch:

May 20 - Financial Times (John Paul Rathbone): "Turkish police detained 22 Istanbul municipality employees on corruption charges…, marking the latest step in a crackdown that began with the arrest of opposition mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu in March… The latest detentions, which included the head of Istanbul's press department, were made under the continuing anti-graft investigation into municipal tenders issued by the alleged 'shadowy' network. Last week, Erdoğan called the probe 'unprecedented'."

Social, Political, Environmental, Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

May 22 - Bloomberg (Jennifer Hiller): "The struggling rooftop solar industry faces a potentially fatal blow after the House of Representatives passed a tougher version of President Trump's expansive tax-and-spending package. The bill sunsets rich renewable energy credits, as expected-but includes more stringent provisions and rollback dates that were seen as especially bleak for rooftop solar. Credits for rooftop solar and battery storage would end this year, while those for larger solar, storage and wind energy projects would end by 2028, instead of a slower phaseout through 2031. 'I think this is basically shutting down the industry,' said Gregg Felton, chief executive of Altus Power, which develops solar projects… 'Nobody will invest any longer in this space with this version of the law.'"

May 19 - New York Times (David Gelles): "When the Trump administration declared two weeks ago that it would largely disregard the economic cost of climate change as it sets policies and regulations, it was just the latest step in a multipronged effort to erase global warming from the American agenda. But President Trump is doing more than just turning a blind eye to the fact that the planet is growing hotter. He is weakening the country's capacity to understand global warming and to prepare for its consequences. The administration has dismantled climate research, firing some of the nation's top scientists, and gutted efforts to chart how fast greenhouse gases are building up in the atmosphere and what that means for the economy, employment, agriculture, health and other aspects of American society. The government will no longer track major sources of greenhouse gases... 'We're not doing that climate change, you know, crud, anymore,' Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins told Fox Business…"

May 22 - Bloomberg (Valerie Volcovici): "The House budget bill that narrowly passed in an early morning vote on Thursday would effectively put the brakes on a clean energy production boom in the United States spurred by tax credits enacted in 2022. The bill to carry out President Donald Trump's 'one big beautiful bill' plan that would further his tax cuts and boost spending on the military and border enforcement would kill Inflation Reduction Act tax credits for clean energy years earlier than initially planned in an earlier draft, rendering them unusable for most projects."

May 22 - Wall Street Journal (Eric Niiler and Scott Patterson): "A downsized National Weather Service is struggling to do more with less as the country heads into a storm season that is expected to see above-normal numbers of hurricanes, tornadoes and wildfires. On Thursday, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration announced it expects 13 to 19 Atlantic storms, with six to 10 reaching hurricane-strength winds… After losing 600 of its 4,000 employees since January amid widespread federal job cuts, the weather service is now scrambling to refill some critical vacancies with staff from NOAA."

May 19 - Bloomberg (Leslie Kaufman): "As climate change worsens extreme weather around the US, floods are increasing the risk of home foreclosure, according to a new report by First Street Technology… A big part of the reason why is that unlike damage from hurricane winds and wildfires, flood damage isn't covered by standard home insurance. Only a small percentage of Americans hold separate flood insurance. First Street analyzed 55 wind, wildfire and flood events that took place in the US between 2000 and 2020. It then compared the foreclosure rates… The analysis found that of 16 wind events, six were followed by a rash of foreclosures. Of 10 wildfires, only one preceded a foreclosure wave. But out of 29 floods, 20 were followed by an unusually high number of foreclosures."

May 22 - Financial Times (Martin Arnold and Kenza Bryan): "Droughts could wipe out nearly 15% of economic output in the Eurozone, the European Central Bank has warned. Eurozone banks have €1.3tn of loans extended to sectors most at risk from potential water shortages - particularly in agriculture, manufacturing, mining and construction… The warning underscores how the central bank is intensifying its focus on the financial risks of climate change, despite a growing political backlash against green policies and pressure from US officials for regulators to water down work in this area. 'Losses related to water scarcity, poor water quality and flood protection emerge as the most critical from a value added perspective,' Frank Elderson, an ECB executive board member, said…"

May 20 - Bloomberg (Natasha White): "Global loss of tropical and boreal forests surged to a record high last year as unprecedented temperatures fueled fires, releasing more than four times the emissions of all air travel in 2023. That's according to the latest annual assessment by Global Forest Watch, a platform run by the nonprofit World Resources Institute... 'It's a global red alert,' Elizabeth Goldman, co-director of Global Forest Watch, said… 'This level of forest loss is unlike anything we've seen in over 20 years.'"

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PDF Solutions: Potential For Fast Growth Has Been Priced In
data.description:
PDF Solutions (PDFS), a provider of data solutions to the semiconductor industry, may appear to be an enigma at first sight. PDFS is growing at a fast clip, faster than the industry as a whole, and the outlook calls for this growth to continue in the coming years. Yet, the stock has done the opposite of what one would have expected with this backdrop, which is to move lower for the better part of the last two years. However, there appears to be a solid reason for the stock to behave this way. Why will be covered next.

The stock and PDFS have diverged by moving in opposite directions

A previous article from November 2024 noted how growth at PDFS had hit a soft patch by slowing down to an essential crawl after a period of strong growth. This was a problem because the stock was priced for fast growth, even though the stock price had basically gone flat at that time. On the other hand, the outlook from PDFS called for strong growth in FY2025, and in the coming years, which implied an end to the soft patch. In the end, the article decided to rate PDFS a hold.

The chart above of the past 12 months shows how the stock was moving sideways, but that changed towards the end of 2024 and especially in the new year. The stock resumed its decline on the way to hitting a new 52-week low of $15.91, on an intraday basis, on April 7. This happened after PDFS suffered blows in the form of the introduction of tariffs following “Liberation Day” and a disappointing Q4 FY2024 report, both of which sent the stock significantly lower.

It is also worth mentioning that the poor performance of the stock goes back even longer than the past 12 months. Note how the overall direction of the stock in the chart above for the past two years seems to be down with lower highs and lower lows. PDFS peaked at $48.02 in July 2023, the culmination of a long rally that played out over several years, but the stock has gradually moved lower since then.

The stock has rebounded in recent weeks thanks to the lowering of trade tensions and the latest quarterly report released during this same period did not stop the bounce. PDFS has nevertheless underperformed within the semiconductor sector with the stock closing at $18.84 on May 21, giving PDFS a loss of 30.4% YTD.

Why the stock’s decline of the past two years may have run its course?

While it is true that the bulls have not had it easy for the last two years or so, there are nonetheless reasons for the bulls to feel optimistic about the road ahead. For instance, the recent rebound in the stock may have been enabled due to support in the charts. Recall how the stock rose from a low of $7.56 in October 2018 to a high of $48.02 in July 2023.

If we then assume the decline in the stock of the past two years is a correction of the preceding move upwards, then the 76.4% Fibonacci retracement of $7.56 to $48.02 is $17.11. Whether by coincidence or not, the stock was only able to close below $17.11 on two trading days before it moved higher. This could be seen as a sign support is in place, although it needs mentioning that the stock can move lower if it succeeds in breaching support.

The case for long PDFS has more arguments to make. While the stock price has moved down, quarterly results have moved in the opposite direction with a return to strong growth in recent quarters. In the latest quarterly report or Q1 FY2025 report, for instance, revenue grew by 16% YoY to $47.8M, making it the third consecutive double-digit or near double-digit YoY increase in quarterly revenue after growth had gone flattish.

This resulted in non-GAAP earnings of $8.11M or $0.21 per share in Q1 FY2025, which is much better than the $0.15 in Q1 FY2024 with a YoY increase of 40%. The much better improvement in the bottom line compared to the top line can be attributed to better margins, which includes a 500 bps YoY increase in non-GAAP gross margin to 77%. This high of a gross margin is actually better than what PDFS aims for in the long run. In terms of GAAP, PDFS reported a loss of $3.03M or $0.08 per share, but this was mostly the result of stock compensation expense totaling $6.60M and $4.34M of non-recurring legal, finance, integration and other costs.

Remember that PDFS completed the acquisition of secureWISE in March, which impacted not just the income statement, but also the balance sheet due to the acquisition cost of $61M in cash. As a consequence, cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments decreased to $54.15M, down from $114.88M a year ago, with total debt of $73.4M.

PDFS expects strong growth ahead

Moreover, not only have recent quarterly results improved, the outlook for FY2025 sees revenue growth picking up even further to result in an increase of 21-23% YoY. This outlook is in part based on tariffs not having that much of an adverse impact. Using these guidelines, PDFS is estimated to earn about $0.90 on revenue of $219M in FY2025. From the Q1 FY2025 earnings call:

“So far, we have not seen any noteworthy change in customer behavior with us as a result of tariffs. Most of our software business, including SaaS, is generally not impacted by tariffs. For the eProbe, tariffs could impact the cost of components shipped into the U.S. However, at this point, we believe it will have only a modest impact on our financial results.

Given our progress in Q1 and despite the macro uncertainty, we reconfirm our revenue growth estimate for the year to be in the range of 21% to 23% when compared with 2024.”

Source: PDFS earnings call

FY2025 growth of 21-23% compares well to the current long-term target financial model from PDFS. PDFS presented its latest Investor Presentation last March, which calls for revenue to grow at a CAGR of more than 20% and a non-GAAP gross margin of more than 75%.

Is PDFS worth buying at current levels?

PDFS seems to have recovered from the recent slump with an acceleration in growth. However, this growth may have already been priced into the stock, in part if not in full. According to one methodology, PDFS needs to have a PEG below one to be deemed undervalued and in order to be worth buying, but it is debatable whether PDFS meets this requirement.

PDFS has earned $0.89 TTM, which confers PDFS a P/E ratio of 21.2x with the stock priced at $18.84 for a market cap of $737.4M. This means PDFS needs to grow non-GAAP EPS at a CAGR significantly greater than 21.2% for at least the next three years in order for PEG to come in significantly below one. This is possible, but by no means a certainty.

In theory, if PDFS manages to hit its goals as laid out in its financial mode, and revenue grows at a CAGR of 20+%, then PDFS stands a solid chance of growing EPS at a CAGR greater than 21.2%, although it is not impossible for bottom-line growth to come in below that of the top line. This can happen, for example, if margins drop.

On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that EPS grew at a slower pace in recent years. Non-GAAP EPS was $0.60 in FY2022, and if we assume FY2025 comes in at $0.90 as estimated earlier, then that translates to a CAGR of 14.5%, or less than what PDFS needs. Granted, PDFS experienced a slump during this period, which affected earnings growth, but it still means PDFS needs to do better than in recent years.

It’s also worth mentioning that earnings growth was even slower on a GAAP basis. Not only that, PDFS only earned $0.04 TTM on a GAAP basis. This not only implies a GAAP P/E ratio of 471x, but it also makes it much less likely that GAAP earnings will grow fast enough for PDFS to be undervalued and worth buying. Remember that GAAP includes stock compensation, unlike non-GAAP, and PDFS makes extensive use of stock compensation.

According to the above methodology, made popular by people like Peter Lynch, PDFS could also be trading at close to fair value. PDFS has earned $0.89 TTM, and if we assume earnings grow in line with the expected growth in revenue, or 21% per year, on average, for the next three years, then fair value is $18.69. This is slightly below where the stock is currently available at $18.84.

There is one more thing worth taking into account as to whether PDFS is worth buying. PDFS keeps issuing new shares, in part because of stock compensation, which potential buyers may not like. As a result, the average number of shares outstanding rose in the past five years from 32.4M in 2019 to 39.0M in 2024, an increase of 20% in the past five years.

Finally, PDFS has been around for more than two decades, yet the balance sheet still shows an accumulated deficit of $97.02M, as of Q1 FY2025. PDFS has lost more money than it has made, something to keep in mind when deciding whether PDFS is worth betting on.

Investor takeaways

PDFS has attributes that are likely to appeal to potential investors. For instance, PDFS provides a range of value-added services to semiconductor companies, which means that if the semiconductor industry grows, PDFS ought to grow along with it, even if there are no guarantees. If someone is bullish on the semiconductor industry and wants exposure in its portfolio to this industry, then PDFS can be used as a play on expected growth in the semiconductor industry.

Furthermore, since PDFS does not directly participate in the manufacture of semiconductors, it is less exposed to the cyclical highs and lows of semiconductor demand. What this means is that PDFS can do well at times when many other semis are hurting from soft demand for new chips. Companies, for example, are likely to value the higher yield PDFS can help achieve, even if semiconductor demand is down. This is another attribute the bulls are likely to appreciate. Its emphasis on services also means that gross margin is higher than many semis, particularly those that are into actual chipmaking.

PDFS expects to grow faster than the industry average at 21-23% in FY2025 and the long-term model believes this level of growth of greater than 20% can be sustained in the coming years. Still, while PDFS has its strengths, the stock got arguably way overvalued when it rallied and multiplied in value from 2019 to 2023. The decline in the stock for the past two years was probably needed from that standpoint.

Growth also hit a soft patch during this time, which likely contributed to the stock’s decline. However, growth has gotten stronger lately and both the FY2025 outlook and the latest financial model sees revenue growing at a 20+% clip until further notice. This should also translate into strong EPS growth, but it is debatable as to how fast exactly. It could reach say 30% at the high end, but it could also come in at 10% per year.

If we assume the average of the two, then, according to one methodology that seeks to determine whether a stock is undervalued, the expected growth has been priced into the stock and PDFS is more or less fairly valued relative to growth. I am thus neutral on PDFS, taking into account all the points raised earlier.

The caveat here is that PDFS could be worth buying if the stock price moves lower, something it has done a lot of in the past two years. The stock has bounced in recent weeks, but a retest of the recent low is possible. If the stock bounces again to the April low, then that would be a signal that support is holding strong, In this case, long PDFS is worth considering as a temporary play.

Bottom line, PDFS seems to be recovering from the recent slowdown in growth, with growth accelerating, possibly into the 20+% range. This growth has been more or less priced into the stock price at this point. PDFS is worth keeping an eye on in case the stock drops again like it did recently, especially if growth meets the targets laid out in the current financial model.

data.title: How Bond Yields Are Reacting To China's Trade War
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Is the bond market flashing a warning that most investors are ignoring?

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Crossmark Global Investment’s Chief Market Strategist, Victoria Fernandez, breaks down what the Moody’s downgrade of US debt really means — and how tariffs, inflation, and political uncertainty are reshaping the fixed income landscape.

Transcript

Mike Larson: What's the key driver for markets here? My next MoneyShow MoneyMasters podcast guest would say, it's the action in bonds. Yes, many investors expected Moody's would eventually get around to downgrading the US's AAA credit rating, and of course, it did last Friday. But trading activity in US and global bond markets, including the test of key treasury yield levels here at home, suggests worries are increasing. Worries about government debt. Worries about budget deficits. Not to mention the ongoing tariff saga and the potential inflationary impact it might have. That's why I'm so excited to be joined by Victoria Fernandez. She's Chief Market Strategist at Crossmark Global Investments, a fixed income specialist with decades of experience in the market, and another all new guest here on the MoneyShow podcast.

Welcome to the podcast, Victoria. Well, Victoria, thank you so much for joining me. I haven't had the pleasure of having you on the podcast before, so I was wondering if you could start by talking a little bit about your background in the markets and what your role is there at Crossmark.

Victoria Fernandez: Absolutely. First, it's a pleasure to be here. Thanks for having me. And my background, really, I was born and raised in Houston. And I went to school here, and I started my career here, and I continue to be here. So really a Texas bred girl for sure. And I began my career working in the fixed income division at a privately held money manager here in Houston, Fayez Sarofim & Company. I was there, I always hate to use years because people can start adding up what my age is. But about 20 years I spent there at that firm doing everything in the fixed income area from research to trading to portfolio management and ended my career there as part of their principals and associates team. I'm helping with some of the management of the firm, but really as it relates to the fixed income side of the business. And I left there in 2012 and moved over to Crossmark. At the time, we were called Capstone. We did go through a name change. So we are Crossmark Global Investments. And I've been here running the fixed income division for the last 13 years.

In addition, because, as you know, in many firms, we wear multiple hats. So I do run the fixed income division. I serve as the portfolio manager for taxable fixed income strategies, but also serve as our Chief Market Strategist. So work very closely with our CEO and CIO, Bob Doll, and other members of the investment team to really kind of set our outlook on what we see going forward in the markets and how we want to position our clients for that.

Mike Larson: Perfect. Well, I appreciate some of that background. I think it sort of flows well into what I'm going to talk about here. And that's the fixed income markets. I mean, I think the big news story, it's obviously what Moody's did late on this past Friday. What are your thoughts on that downgrade and in terms of what it might mean in either the short term or the long term?

Victoria Fernandez: Yeah, it's interesting. The bond market really is kind of driving the equity markets as well right now. And when we look at that downgrade that happened, I was actually watching all week long, waiting to see some large reaction in yields, and I didn't get it to the extent that I thought we would. And then I had to sit back and go, well, look, this is the third rating agency to do this, right? It's been a very long time. We had S&P, we had Fitch. Moody's is finally coming to the party. So maybe it wasn't a huge surprise to a lot of investors or to the marketplace in general.

And the items that they mentioned for the downgrade, these are items that we've all been talking about forever. Interest expense. I mean, how many times have we talked about interest expense? Once it gets over 14% of revenues, austerity should kick in, and we're at like 18% now. So we've been talking about that for a while. We talked about it at the MoneyShow in Dallas with people there.

And so, I don't think it was as large of a surprise. We know we have a deficit issue. We know Congress is working on the tax bill. So all of this has been really kind of front of mind. And I have a feeling that maybe that was part of the reason that we didn't see even a larger move in yields.

Now, yields did move up. We had kind of a parallel shift on the curve. So yields moved up across the curve. But I don't think it was all because of the Moody's element. I think there's probably some inflation expectations that are getting higher. We've seen that with the University of Michigan. We've seen it with the New York Fed numbers. So I think you add the downgrade along with inflation expectations concern around growth, and you're going to get higher yields. And that's not just here, right? We're seeing it globally as well.

Mike Larson: Yeah, I'm glad you mentioned that. I mean, it's kind of interesting. When you spoke in Dallas, it was right amidst the sort of equity market volatility storm. Now, as we're speaking, 30s are sort of testing that 5% level again yield, 10s are back above 4.5% give or take. Do you think we're going to see more of this kind of bond market volatility, if not related to the downgrade - which, as you mentioned, was kind of anticipated - but the bigger-picture issues, debts, deficits, inflation, and so on?

Victoria Fernandez: Absolutely, I think we will. I mean, those bond vigilantes are kind of waiting there in the wings, right? I think they are ready to make their voice be heard and if they're seeing things that they don't like. And perhaps, again, that's part of the move that we've seen, but we could be kind of in a consolidation phase here - and not a tight range, but a larger range for yields. So let's just talk about 10-year yields. I mean, we are at a 4.50% this morning. So, are we going to be in a range of maybe 4.25-4.60% to 4.25-4.75% for the foreseeable future? I think we could be. And as you know, some of the moves that we've seen in yields over the past 18 months or so, I mean, we could go 50, 75 basis points in a very short period of time.

I'm not saying that that can't happen, but I have a feeling that the bond market has priced in a lot of the uncertainty that we have there. And so we'll probably move back and forth, but I'm thinking our range is going to be maybe 50 basis points total. So maybe you get 10-15 basis points of movement instead of that 50-75 that we've been seeing. But the volatility is going to be there until we, I think, get through a lot of the policy uncertainty, the monetary uncertainty. There's just a lot going on out there, and we need some answers on that before I think people feel confident to put their money to work.

Although I will say at a 4.50% on a 10-year and a 5% on a 30-year, it's a little advantageous for someone to go in and start locking in some of that income, as yields probably will move lower over the next 12 months.

Mike Larson: You mentioned the “U” word, uncertainty. It's kind of interesting, one of your previous appearances on TV the other day was talking about the dichotomy between hard and soft data, uncertainty being cited but people still spending, job markets still being okay. What are your thoughts on how that all shakes out? I mean, are we going to see this big transition from soft data being weak into hard data being weak, or do you think it's more of a tempest in a teapot?

Victoria Fernandez: Yeah, you would expect that that soft data would flow through to hard data. Soft data is more kind of about expectations and what people think is going to happen. Hard data is more kind of the confirmation of that. So it usually starts in the soft data and then bleeds into hard data. And when it hits that hard data, that's when the Fed starts to take action. That's when they see it in their numbers that they've been waiting for. How long have we heard they're data-dependent? They're waiting on that. But I find it interesting that we are not seeing that flow through as quickly as I would have expected.

The soft data has been really weak. And I actually think the labor market is probably weaker, when you look at some of the underlying trends, than what we're seeing in the hard data. And so the question is, how does that flow through? Is it not doing it because there was such a pull forward prior to tariffs?

So I can sit there and say in a survey to you in soft data, I'm very concerned about inflation. I'm concerned that I could get laid off if growth really comes down, if profit margins get squeezed at corporations, I could get laid off. But yet, my actions could say, wait a minute, I need to buy this item, and it's probably going to be 15-20% higher 6 months from now. Let me go ahead and buy it now.

So actions and words have not aligned. I would assume at some point that's going to happen. And maybe it's a little give on both sides. Maybe it's not completely flow through to hard data. Maybe soft data gets a little better and hard data starts to weaken, and they kind of meet in the middle.

Mike Larson: Okay, fair enough. Let's shift to equities here. I mean, we've had the big sell-off, big rally, and now we're kind of chopping around a little bit trying to figure out what's going to happen next. What are your thoughts on that score? I mean, where does the market go from here? Any sectors that you think are a good way to play in the next few months, next couple of quarters?

Victoria Fernandez: I have to say - and I am a bond girl, right - so I kind of lean towards the more cautious, more conservative nature of most investors. But it does make me a little wary when I look at how quickly we came back from those lows that we saw back in April. I mean, it was such a quick move back that I feel like we've got to have some consolidation.

And maybe we're starting to have that right now. The S&P keeps bumping up against some of its resistance levels. So maybe we're going to start to consolidate a little bit here. I would look at sectors that have been in uptrends even during all the volatility that we have seen. So, I know some of these names have had pullbacks, but their uptrends remain.

So you look at financials, they continue to do well. So maybe dive in a little bit, look for certain names as they pull back in the market that you can add. Industrials have actually started to make some relative highs, so they're starting to perform better. So that's a positive sign. Unfortunately, being in Houston, the energy sector has just been continually weak. They're in a downtrend. So it makes it, you know, a lot of people say, well, you step in and buy the dip when they come down automatically, but you don't want to buy a dip when they're in a downtrend, right? So we'd still be a little bit cautious there, but utilities continue to do well also.

And so you're looking at things like that - industrials, some utilities, some financials. I think you can find some names there to do well, but I'm just not seeing enough evidence in the internal momentum of the market on a sustained basis to tell me that we're just going to go up and to the right from here. I think we'll continue to have some of this churning for at least the next few months.

Mike Larson: Got it. And thoughts on fixed income positioning. I mean, if you're sitting down with someone and kind of talking about what parts of the fixed income market look attractive or as coping strategies, what would you suggest there?

Victoria Fernandez: Yes, so we've actually really liked the treasuries as of late, mostly because there is the anticipation that the Fed will cut rates. Now, we've said there's probably just 1 or 2 cuts this year because it's going to take a little bit for some of the elements of the tariffs and things to flow through, but we'll probably get more cuts next year. So you could position yourself for some cuts on the shorter end of the curve, and yields are nice and attractive there. And then we would barbell out to the longer end of the curve and lock in some rates like we were talking about.

You can get some nice investment grade corporates. We don't like to go into high yield too often, especially if we're concerned about growth going forward, so we would stick with investment grade. They've widened out some this year. There's been a little bit of tightening, but they're still wider than where they were at the beginning of the year. So you can go in and pick up some investment grade corporates, pick up some treasuries. And we would do kind of the short end and the longer end of the curve. The belly of the curve, that kind of 5-year mark, has been a little expensive. That can change obviously, but that's how we're positioning for our clients.

Mike Larson: Got it, perfect. And within the equity space, you mentioned a couple of sectors. Are you comfortable with naming a name or two of individual stocks, or you’d rather stick with sectors?

Victoria Fernandez: No, we can talk a little bit about names, and I think when you look at financials, Goldman Sachs (GS) is a name that we have really liked. We anticipate that as you have, kind of, the deregulation elements from this administration as we get the tax bill going through - we're assuming these things are going to happen - coupled with tariffs, it was always kind of a broader picture of what we were looking at. You're going to get some positive movements, you're going to get some M&A activity going on, and Goldman would benefit from that. And so, Goldman, we think, is a great name in the financials space.

And we also like - even though tech has not been in a solid uptrend - a name like Cisco (CSCO) has actually been doing really well individually, and you're not paying too high of a price for it. They're changing the way that they do a lot of their business, and some of their revenue now is coming more from software and elements and services that they have. So I think you can kind of eliminate some of the volatility of the Mag 7 that you see and find in other tech names. And so, Cisco would be a good name that we would like as well.

Mike Larson: Perfect, great. Victoria, you're going to be speaking at our Las Vegas event in July. Obviously, it's far out, so we kind of have to name these topics ahead of time, but yours is going to be fewer tailwinds and more tail risks. If somebody attends that event, what are some of the things you think you're going to talk about? What are these tail risks? What are a couple of things people need to be aware of as we roll into the back half of the year?

Victoria Fernandez: Yeah, so we've mentioned a few of them already, and we'll dive a little bit deeper. And I do have to say a caveat, obviously when it comes to July, that title could change, right, depending on what's happened going on. But some of the tail risks that we have been seeing - and I talked about the weakness of the labor market and how we think it's probably weaker than what some people are seeing. So we think that's an issue. When it comes to tariffs, I am of the camp that, look, we are only in a 90-day pause. I am by no means confident that we hit the 90 days. Remember, there's two separate 90-day pauses that we're in, right? I am under -- I'm not under the impression that that is just going to hit 90 days and everything is going to be great. We could see tariffs go right back up again. So there's a concern there.

And I also have concerns around China - not just the tariffs, but I think some of maybe the Middle East action that we have seen over the past few days in deals when President Trump was there could be setting it up for the administration to go back and take a harder stance on China once again. So those are just a few of the things that I think could be tail risks, along with geopolitical events, obviously there's a lot of that as well. So those are a few of them. There's more that we'll discuss in Las Vegas.

Mike Larson: Perfect. Victoria, I want to thank you so much for joining me for this session.

Victoria Fernandez: My pleasure, thanks for having me.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Over 6% Yield And A Deep Discount: Is United Parcel Service The Best Contrarian Play In 2025?
data.description:
Here’s where I stand: I think the market’s overreacting and tossing out United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS) with everything else. The stock’s taken a beating—down over 35% in the last year, and it’s not far from its 52-week low. Most of this sell-off is tied to some real challenges (no sugarcoating that) but to me, the current price feels like Wall Street is only looking at the negatives and completely ignoring what’s actually changing under the hood.

The big narrative lately is “Amazon’s pulling packages, UPS is laying people off, restructuring—and it’s all looking messy.” Yeah, that’s happening. But dig a little deeper, and you see a company aggressively slashing costs ($3.5 billion on deck for this year), shifting its focus to healthcare logistics and small business customers (which have fatter margins) and still raking in enough cash to cover a nearly 7% dividend yield. So if you’re willing to look past the next couple of rough quarters, I honestly think there’s a lot more upside than downside here.

Mispricing

I really think the market’s missing the bigger picture with UPS. Everybody’s hung up on Amazon shifting volume over to FedEx and what that means for total package numbers. But let’s face it: Amazon was a pretty low-margin customer for UPS. Yeah, the volume looks nice on paper, but they weren’t making much money on those packages. Losing Amazon stings, but it also gives UPS a chance to clean house—close less efficient facilities, shrink their headcount and really focus on the business lines that actually move the needle.

Right now, the stock is trading at valuations we haven’t seen in years—P/E around 14, EV/EBITDA just under 9. It’s rare to see UPS this cheap unless everyone’s convinced things will get permanently worse. I don’t buy that.

What Management is Saying

If you read through the 10-Q and listen to what management’s actually saying, they’re not in denial about the rough patch. They straight-up call 2025 a “reset year” and they’re not promising a quick fix. The message is more like: “Look, we’re biting the bullet on costs right now, but we’re setting ourselves up for better margins on the other side of this.”

There’s a lot of focus on automation, consolidating the U.S. network and moving into healthcare and international. The Andlauer Healthcare deal (that $1.6 billion acquisition) isn’t just for show—they’re serious about making healthcare a much bigger part of the pie. They also keep talking about improving returns on invested capital, even if that means earnings look ugly for a few quarters. It’s not the kind of language you’d expect if they were just trying to paper over problems; there’s a sense they’re willing to take a hit now to come out stronger in 2026 and beyond.

Valuation: Let’s Get Into the Numbers

Right now, UPS is at about $96 a share. Here’s what that gets you:

  • P/E: 14-ish

  • EV/EBITDA: Just under 9

  • EV/Sales: A little over 1

  • Dividend Yield: ~6.8%

Compared to history? UPS usually trades closer to 18–20x earnings when things are “normal.” FedEx and DHL trade in a similar range. Yet, UPS still throws off better cash flow than FedEx and pays a much higher dividend than either.

If I run a rough DCF and assume they limp through 2025 but get back to low-single-digit revenue growth and steady margins, fair value comes out in the $120–130 range (so we’re talking 25–35% upside just to get back to “average”). If things go better than expected—like healthcare really takes off or the cost cuts overdeliver—I can easily see $140–150. The bear case? If everything goes sideways—more volume drops, margin compression, tariffs bite—you might see the stock hang out around $80–85. But honestly, at this price, a lot of that’s already priced in.

News & Sentiment: What’s Actually Moving the Stock

If you’ve followed the headlines, it’s been a rollercoaster. UPS announced they’re cutting 20,000 jobs and shutting some facilities—initially the market hated it, but it’s actually the sort of medicine they need. Losing the Amazon business was another gut punch and FedEx jumped on that immediately. Tariffs are another wild card, with investors basically throwing every China-related fear at all the big logistics stocks.

Still, there’s some good news getting lost in the noise. UPS just bought Andlauer Healthcare, which should help offset Amazon losses with higher-margin business. They beat Q1 earnings expectations, posted record-high revenue per package, and kept the dividend rock solid. It feels like sentiment is as bad as it’s been in years—almost like nobody believes in a turnaround. But if you look at the last couple of weeks, the stock stopped sliding and actually started to stabilize. That’s usually a sign sellers are getting exhausted.

Risks

Let’s call it like it is: UPS isn’t some risk-free, “set and forget” stock—especially not right now. There’s a lot that could go sideways, and some of it is out of management’s hands.

The biggie right now is whether UPS can actually deliver on this $3.5 billion cost-cutting promise. They’re closing buildings, automating the network and letting a lot of people go. That’s a ton of moving parts, and it’s not hard to imagine things getting messy—maybe they save less than planned, or maybe service takes a hit and big customers get annoyed. It’s happened before in this industry, and it can get expensive, fast.

Then there’s the whole Amazon situation. Amazon wasn’t just another client—it was their biggest. Now, with Amazon pulling more volume in-house (and FedEx licking its chops for any leftovers), UPS is scrambling to fill that gap with new healthcare and small business customers. If those don’t ramp up quickly enough or don’t bring in the same profits, the whole network gets less efficient. Suddenly, what looked like a “reset year” could drag on a lot longer.

Don’t forget labor. Most of UPS’s U.S. workforce is unionized, and any hint of a strike—or even just tough contract talks—tends to spook investors. Plus, they’ve got a huge pile of pension and healthcare obligations, which are basically locked in whether business is booming or not.

And then, you’ve got the economy and regulators. If there’s a global trade hiccup, more tariffs, or just a slowdown in e-commerce, UPS feels it right away. Throw in the wildcards—fuel price spikes, interest rates, or currency swings—and suddenly margins look a lot shakier. Oh, and the end of that “de minimis” tax break on cheap Chinese goods? That’s still a giant question mark for how much cross-border business actually slows down.

Lastly, tech is both an opportunity and a risk. As UPS leans more on automation and software, the potential for a big IT melt down or even a cyberattack gets a lot higher. That’s the trade-off for trying to modernize.

If they don’t deliver on execution, there’s a real risk the dividend (which is generous right now) comes into question. At these payout levels, a rough patch in free cash flow means they’d have to choose between protecting the dividend or keeping the balance sheet healthy. I’d expect some drama if it comes to that.

So, bottom line: UPS has its work cut out for it. The list of risks is real and pretty public at this point. You have to believe management can pull off this turnaround for the stock to work from here. If they can’t, it’s probably dead money—or worse—for a while.

How Could This Play Out?

  • Bear case: Amazon leaves a bigger hole than expected, UPS can’t save as much as hoped, and tariffs pinch margins even harder. The stock could hang out in the $80s. Not fun, but a lot of pain is already in the price.

  • Base case: Cost cuts start to work, healthcare and SMB business slowly replace lost Amazon volume, and the market realizes things aren’t as dire as they look. Shares drift back to $120–130 over the next 12–24 months.

  • Bull case: UPS absolutely nails its cost savings, new growth areas take off, and sentiment swings the other way. We could be talking about $140–150, especially if FedEx and the sector in general catch a tailwind.

My Bottom Line

If you’re looking for a place to hide for the next quarter, this probably isn’t it. But if you can stomach some near-term noise and want a shot at both value and yield, I genuinely think UPS at $90–100 is a solid buy. I’d probably leg in slowly—no need to go all-in at once given the volatility. The stock could be dead money for a few months while the headlines are still grim, but longer-term, I see a clear path to recovery.

Honestly, setups like this don’t come along that often: a blue-chip brand, a beaten-down price, a yield that’s hard to find anywhere else and the kind of operational shakeup that could make the next few years look very different from the last. If management executes, this could end up looking like a textbook rebound play.

If you’re an income investor, or you just like buying when the crowd’s scared, this is exactly the sort of setup you wait for. I’m calling UPS a Buy—and for anyone with patience, maybe even a “Strong Buy” if you’re okay with a little messiness in the near term. I wouldn’t wait forever to build a position. Opportunities like this tend to snap back fast once the narrative shifts.
